post.title: The Hidden Data Engine Behind Every Great Real Estate Map – What Brokers, Developers, and Investors Need to Know
post.author: patrick sang
post.excerpt:
You’ve used GIS to find parcels, check flood zones, or run a drive‑time analysis. The map looks simple – colourful polygons, a search bar, maybe a slider for school ratings. But behind that map is a machine of data engineering that determines whether your information is accurate, up‑to‑date, and fast enough for live use.

In 20 years of real estate tech, I’ve seen the same failures: a map that shows last year’s parcel boundaries, a query that takes 30 seconds to return comps, or a flood overlay that is misaligned by 50 metres. All those problems are data engineering problems, not GIS software problems. Here’s what you need to understand.

1. Parcel Geometry Is Never “Perfect” – And That’s an Engineering Challenge
Counties usually publish parcel data as Shapefiles or GeoPackages. But those files often contain invalid polygons: self‑intersecting boundaries, tiny slivers (area <0.001 m²), or missing rings. When raw data is loaded directly into a map, those errors can cause:

Disappearing parcels (the renderer skips them)

Wrong area calculations (a sliver makes area 0.01 acres instead of 2 acres)

Failed spatial joins (parcel won’t intersect its own flood zone because geometry is broken)

Data engineers run geometry validation and cleaning as the first step in the pipeline. Using libraries like shapely or PostGIS ST_MakeValid(), they fix the violations before any analysis. A good pipeline logs invalid geometries and alerts the operator – often indicating a title survey problem that needs human review.

What you should ask your GIS vendor: “Do you run geometry validation on all parcels? What percentage are automatically fixed, and how are exceptions handled?”

2. Coordinate Systems – The Silent Killer of Map Overlays
A flood map from FEMA uses WGS84 (EPSG:4326) – latitude/longitude. County parcels often come in State Plane (e.g., EPSG:26986 for Massachusetts). If you simply overlay them without reprojection, parcels will appear shifted by hundreds of metres – sometimes in the wrong water body.

The engineering solution is automatic CRS transformation on every ingest. A good data pipeline:

Reads the source CRS from the file (if missing, asks user or assumes a default)

Transforms all geometries to a uniform analysis CRS (often a projected coordinate system that preserves area, like State Plane)

Stores the original CRS as metadata for audit

For web maps, the final step transforms to Web Mercator (EPSG:3857), which is what Leaflet, Mapbox, and Google Maps use. That transformation happens inside the database or tile server – invisible to you.

Red flag: If you ever see a real‑estate map where parcels and satellite imagery don’t line up, the CRS transformation was omitted or done incorrectly.

3. Why “Find Similar Sales” Takes 0.3 Seconds (or 30 Seconds)
The operation “find all parcels sold in last 6 months within 500 metres of this parcel” is a distance join. Without a spatial index, the database must compare every pair of parcels – O(n²) complexity. For a county with 500,000 parcels, that’s 250 billion comparisons – impossible in real time.

Data engineers build spatial indexes (R‑trees, GiST indexes in PostGIS). An R‑tree organises geometries into nested bounding boxes, allowing the database to quickly discard far‑away parcels. The same query goes from 30 seconds to 0.3 seconds.

But there’s more: they also denormalise common queries. For example, if every user asks for “parcel + school district + flood zone”, they might create a materialised view that pre‑joins those layers nightly. The dashboard then reads that flat table – even faster.

Ask your platform provider: “Do you use spatial indexes? Do you pre‑join common layers for performance?”

4. The Nightly Parcel Update – Why Fresh Data Is Hard
Counties update parcel boundaries and APN tables on different schedules: daily, weekly, or monthly. The naive approach is to delete all data and reload – but that’s slow (hours) and wasteful.

Data engineers use Change Data Capture (CDC). They compute a hash (fingerprint) of each parcel’s geometry + attributes. When a new county export arrives, they compare hashes:

Hash unchanged → ignore

Hash changed → update existing record

New APN appears → insert

APN missing (deleted parcel) → soft‑delete or archive

This reduces the processing window from hours to minutes and ensures that the map doesn’t blink (no full teardown). It also allows a history table – you can see what the parcel looked like last month, which is essential for tax appeals and litigation.

5. Vector Tiles: The Secret to a Snappy Web Map
Old‑style web maps use raster tiles – the server draws a PNG image for every zoom level. If you have 20 layers (parcels, zoning, easements, comps, etc.), the server must render those images on‑the‑fly, which is slow and rigid.

Modern real‑estate dashboards use vector tiles (MVT) . A data engineer pre‑processes all layers into small, compact tiles containing the raw geometry and attributes (but simplified). The user’s browser downloads those tiles and renders them instantly. You can change the styling (e.g., colour parcels by school rating) without asking the server for new images.

The engineering challenge is tile generation – simplifying geometries so tiles aren’t huge but still look sharp. Tools like Tippecanoe or PostGIS’s ST_AsMVT() are used to create tile pyramids that are then served via a CDN.

Result: A map with millions of parcels that pans and zooms as smoothly as Google Maps.

Bottom Line for Real Estate Pros
The map is just the tip of the iceberg. Underneath is a stack of geometry validation, CRS transformation, spatial indexing, CDC pipelines, and vector tiles. When you evaluate a real‑estate GIS product or build your own, invest in data engineering early. It’s the difference between “that map is cool” and “I trust this data for a $10M decision.”

post.title: Building a Production‑Grade Real Estate Parcel Pipeline: Architecture, Pitfalls, and Code Examples
post.author: patrick sang
post.excerpt:
Real estate GIS looks straightforward: a polygon for each parcel, rows of assessor data, a few regulatory overlays. Then you try to build a pipeline that updates daily, scales to 10+ million parcels across multiple counties, and serves a live dashboard. Welcome to the chaos.

Here is the architecture I’ve refined over several large‑scale real‑estate data platforms. I’ll include concrete code patterns and the mistakes to avoid.

Step 1: Ingestion – Leave Shapefiles Behind
Most counties still publish Shapefiles or even worse – PDF maps. For those that provide machine‑readable data, accept GeoPackage, GeoJSON, or FileGDB. Better yet, convert immediately to GeoParquet. GeoParquet is columnar, compressed, and works wonderfully with cloud analytical engines.

Ingestion pattern (Python + DuckDB):

python
import duckdb
import requests

# Download county parcel GeoPackage
url = "https://gis.county.gov/parcels.gpkg"
r = requests.get(url)
with open("raw/parcels.gpkg", "wb") as f:
    f.write(r.content)

# Convert to GeoParquet, partitioning by county and year
duckdb.execute("""
  INSTALL spatial; LOAD spatial;
  COPY (
    SELECT geom, apn, land_use, acres, last_sale_date, last_sale_price,
           'alameda' AS county, 2026 AS year
    FROM ST_Read('raw/parcels.gpkg')
  ) TO 's3://real-estate-lake/parcels/alameda/year=2026/data.parquet'
  WITH (FORMAT PARQUET, PARTITION_BY (county, year));
""")
Why this works: Parquet pushdown predicates – when you later query only Alameda county and year 2026, the engine reads only that file. Also, DuckDB’s spatial extension processes the geometry without loading everything into memory.

Step 2: Geometry Validation – Fail Loudly
Invalid geometries are inevitable. Run validation before loading into your main database.

sql
-- PostGIS validation
CREATE TABLE parcels_staging AS
SELECT apn, geom, ST_IsValidReason(geom) AS validity_reason
FROM raw_parcels;

-- Only pass valid ones to final table
INSERT INTO parcels_final (apn, geom)
SELECT apn, ST_MakeValid(geom)
FROM parcels_staging
WHERE ST_IsValid(geom);

-- Log invalid parcels for manual inspection
INSERT INTO invalid_parcels_log
SELECT apn, validity_reason, current_timestamp
FROM parcels_staging
WHERE NOT ST_IsValid(geom);
Pitfall: ST_MakeValid() can change polygon area (e.g., remove slivers). Always store both original and fixed geometry, and compute area difference. If area changes >1%, flag for human review – it may indicate a boundary error in the source.

Step 3: Conflation – Merging Adjacent County Parcels
When a property lies across county lines, you have two separate parcel records with mismatched boundaries. Conflation solves this.

Simplified conflation workflow using PostGIS:

sql
-- Identify candidate conflicts: overlapping parcels from two counties
SELECT a.apn AS apn_a, b.apn AS apn_b,
       ST_Area(ST_Intersection(a.geom, b.geom)) / ST_Area(ST_Union(a.geom, b.geom)) AS overlap_ratio
FROM parcels_county_a a, parcels_county_b b
WHERE ST_Intersects(a.geom, b.geom)
  AND ST_Area(ST_Intersection(a.geom, b.geom)) > 0;

-- For high overlap (>0.95), assume same parcel: merge attributes with rule
-- Rule: county A APN is primary, but use county B's land_use if A is null
CREATE VIEW merged_parcels AS
SELECT COALESCE(a.apn, b.apn) AS apn,
       COALESCE(a.geom, b.geom) AS geom,
       COALESCE(a.land_use, b.land_use) AS land_use,
       a.last_sale_price AS price_a,
       b.last_sale_price AS price_b
FROM parcels_county_a a
FULL OUTER JOIN parcels_county_b b ON ST_Intersects(a.geom, b.geom)
WHERE ST_Area(ST_Intersection(a.geom, b.geom)) / ST_Area(ST_Union(a.geom, b.geom)) > 0.95;
For low overlap but continuous boundary (e.g., a road dividing two counties), use edge‑matching – snap vertices of one county’s parcels to the other’s boundary line. A tool like ogr2ogr with -snap or custom PostGIS ST_Snap is required.

Step 4: Spatial Indexing – Query Performance That Scales
After loading a clean geometry table, create the spatial index immediately.

sql
CREATE INDEX idx_parcels_geom ON parcels_final USING GIST (geom);
For even faster point‑in‑polygon queries (e.g., geocoding a list of addresses), consider a grid‑based index or use PostGIS 3+ with BRIN indexes for very large, static tables.

Example: high‑performance nearest neighbour query
Finding the 10 nearest sold parcels to a given parcel:

sql
SELECT p2.apn, p2.last_sale_price, p2.geom
FROM parcels_final p1, parcels_final p2
WHERE p1.apn = '123-456-789'
  AND p1.apn != p2.apn
  AND p2.last_sale_date > '2025-01-01'
ORDER BY p1.geom <-> p2.geom
LIMIT 10;
The <-> operator uses the spatial index to compute distance without exhaustive scan.

Step 5: Incremental Updates with CDC
Assuming county publishes a full dump nightly, implement CDC with hash comparison.

Step‑by‑step in Python with hashlib and PostGIS:

Load new dump into temp table parcels_new.

Compute SHA256 hash of geometry + all key attributes for each row.

Compare with last_hash column in parcels_final (main table).

Perform three operations:

sql
-- Inserts
INSERT INTO parcels_final (apn, geom, attributes, last_hash)
SELECT apn, geom, attributes, new_hash
FROM parcels_new n
WHERE NOT EXISTS (SELECT 1 FROM parcels_final f WHERE f.apn = n.apn);

-- Updates
UPDATE parcels_final f
SET geom = n.geom,
    attributes = n.attributes,
    last_hash = n.new_hash,
    last_updated = now()
FROM parcels_new n
WHERE f.apn = n.apn
  AND f.last_hash != n.new_hash;

-- Deletes (soft‑delete)
UPDATE parcels_final SET is_deleted = true, deleted_at = now()
WHERE apn NOT IN (SELECT apn FROM parcels_new);
Optimisation: Use a hash index on apn for faster lookups: CREATE INDEX ON parcels_final (apn);.

Step 6: Expose as Vector Tiles for the Frontend
Never serve raw geometries directly to a web browser. Instead, build a tile service.

Using pg_tileserv (lightweight PostGIS tile server):

yaml
# config.toml
listen = ":7800"
database_url = "postgres://user:pass@localhost/db"
Define a tile function in SQL:

sql
CREATE FUNCTION public.parcels_zxy(z integer, x integer, y integer)
RETURNS bytea AS $$
  SELECT ST_AsMVT(tile, 'parcels', 4096, 'geom')
  FROM (
    SELECT apn, land_use, ST_AsMVTGeom(geom, ST_TileEnvelope(z, x, y), 4096, 64, true) AS geom
    FROM parcels_final
    WHERE geom && ST_TileEnvelope(z, x, y)
  ) AS tile
$$ LANGUAGE sql STABLE;
The tile envelope function (ST_TileEnvelope) uses the spatial index automatically. Pre‑baking tiles (with Tippecanoe) is even faster for static or slowly changing datasets.

Monitoring and Alerting
A production pipeline needs health checks:

Row count diff – if new dump has <90% of expected parcel count, alert.

Geometry validity rate – alert if >1% of new parcels are invalid.

CDC runtime – warn if >30 minutes.

Tile generation freshness – last tile update time should be within 1 hour of last successful ETL.

Example Prometheus metric (Python + prometheus_client):

python
from prometheus_client import Gauge

parcel_count_gauge = Gauge('parcel_total_count', 'Total active parcels')
invalid_geom_gauge = Gauge('parcel_invalid_geom_count', 'Invalid geometries in staging')

# After ETL
parcel_count_gauge.set(select count(*) from parcels_final where is_deleted=false)
Final Architecture Summary
text
County FTP / S3 ──► Raw GeoParquet (data lake)
                         │
                         ▼ (DuckDB / Spark)
                 Geometry validation & CRS normalization
                         │
                         ▼ (CDC diff)
                 PostGIS (production) ──► GIST index
                         │
          ┌──────────────┼──────────────┐
          ▼              ▼              ▼
    Vector tiles    Analytics (dbt)   API (pg_featureserv)
    (pg_tileserv)   (denormalised views)
          │              │
          ▼              ▼
     Web dashboard   Real estate apps
This pipeline scales to tens of millions of parcels, updates daily in under 15 minutes, and serves interactive maps with sub‑second tile loads.

Remember: Real‑estate data engineering is about trust. Every broken polygon, every misaligned CRS, every slow query erodes trust. Build for correctness first, then optimise. And always, always keep a copy of the raw county data – you will need it for audits and appeals.
